html:
XIII

The girl with the pink hair, languorous lashes gummy with an overdose of mascara, and ugly black muffins over her ears, was informing Mr.¬†Brent that a Doctor Merrick was in the lobby and wished to see himвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ No, he hadnвАЩt said.

вАЬMr.¬†Brent is dressing for dinner and canвАЩt come down,вАЭ she said coyly, with one hand over the mouthpiece. вАЬHe says can you tell him on the phone what you want. Just step into booth number two, please.вАЭ

вАЬTell him IвАЩll be right up.вАЭ

She relayed the message, while the tall young man with the shoulders of a discus-thrower and the tapering waist of a fencer tapped the high counter in front of the switchboard with slender, impatient fingers.

вАЬHe says it wonвАЩt be convenient,вАЭ she reported, visibly reluctant to transmit the blunt discourtesy.

вАЬWhatвАЩs his room number?вАЭ inquired Doctor Merrick, unruffled.

вАЬThree hundred and seventy-eight; butвБ†вАФhe said he couldnвАЩt see you, you know.вАЭ

вАЬHere, boy,вАЭ to the bellhop at his elbow, вАЬput those bags in check, and then show me up to three hundred and seventy-eight.вАЭ

Mr.¬†Brent was not dressing for dinner. He was packing a trunk; and chairs, bed and table were littered with clothing, papers, books, toilet articles and mussy linen. The room was in great disorder. It was with a very surly scowl that he opened the door to inspect his visitor.

вАЬMerrickвБ†вАФyou say? Never heard of you. WhatвАЩs wanted?вАЭ He planted his short, stocky bulk defiantly in the doorway, hands on hips.

вАЬAsk me in and IвАЩll tell you,вАЭ said Merrick quietly.

Brent reluctantly stepped back.

вАЬOh, very well,вАЭ he snapped, peevishly. вАЬBut make it peppy. IвАЩm busyвБ†вАФas you seeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Thought IвАЩd sent word down that I didnвАЩt care to be bothered.вАЭ

The loud-checked coat that matched the trousers he had on was lifted from the back of a chair and tossed on the bed.

вАЬSit downвБ†вАФif you want to.вАЭ

Young Merrick ignored the sour invitation and proceeded to state his errand.

вАЬI live in Detroit where I am associated with Brightwood Hospital.вАЭ

BrentвАЩs face, pallid, and bristly with two daysвАЩ beard, went a shade paler.

вАЬYeah?вАЭ

вАЬYou may recall that Brightwood Hospital was brought into prominence by your cousinвАЩs late husbandвБ†вАФDoctor Hudson.вАЭ

вАЬWellвБ†вАФand then what?вАЭ growled Brent, insolently.

вАЬIt came to our attention, about thirty days ago, that Mrs.¬†HudsonвБ†вАФnow in Italy, as you knowвБ†вАФwas obliged to dispose of her interest in the hospital.вАЭ

вАЬAnd howвАЩs that any of your damn business?вАЭ demanded Brent, stepping toward Merrick belligerently. вАЬYouвАЩre just a doctor, arenвАЩt you? CouldnвАЩt she sell her hospital stock, if she wanted to, without consulting you?вАЭ

вАЬQuite true,вАЭ replied Merrick, determined not to lose his temper. вАЬShe had no occasion to confide in me, and didnвАЩt. But if we are interested in her welfare, I should think that might meet with your approval. YouвАЩve been managing her affairs, havenвАЩt you?вАЭ

вАЬYeah! And I donвАЩt need any help!вАЭ

вАЬI happen to know that you do. ThatвАЩs what I came here to talk about.вАЭ

вАЬAnd what makes you so powerfully interested in my cousin?вАЭ Brent sneered. вАЬTrying to get your fingers on her money?вАЭ

The fingers were restless.

вАЬI advise you not to presume too far on my patience, Brent.вАЭ

вАЬWhen you get too impatient, you can leave!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ You want to marry my cousin, I supposeвБ†вАФbut have to make sure, first, that she has plenty to keep you!вАЭ

вАЬJust for the moment,вАЭ cautioned Merrick, вАЬweвАЩll not be talking about Mrs.¬†Hudson. WeвАЩre going to talk about you!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And that Northwestern Copper stock!вАЭ

вАЬWhat do you mean, you low-down, sneaking spy?вАЭ

вАЬI mean that in the last twelve months youвАЩve lost upwards of one hundred thousand dollars on the ticker and the poniesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ That last big flyer in oilвБ†вАФalong in May, wasnвАЩt it?вБ†вАФwiped out the Northwestern Copper completely!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ IвАЩm here, as Mrs.¬†HudsonвАЩs friend, to find out exactly what you propose to do about it.вАЭ

BrentвАЩs face was livid. He stamped to the door and threw it open.

вАЬNowвБ†вАФout you go, damn you!вБ†вАФor IвАЩll call the house detective!вАЭ

Merrick turned to the untidy desk and took up the telephone.

вАЬIвАЩll save you the trouble,вАЭ he said quietly, lifting the receiver.

вАЬPut that down!вАЭ screamed Brent, slamming the door.

Merrick smiled and obeyed.

вАЬYouвАЩre not anxious to talk to a detective, Brent. But youвАЩre going to talk to me! Do you want to come clean on all this, now, and tell me about itвБ†вАФor do I have to break you in two over that table? I can do it, you know; and IвАЩd like to!вАЭ

Blind with fury, Brent lunged savagely with his fist. Stepping aside to let it go by, Merrick caught the shaking wrist in a vice-like grip of his left hand. With the other, he gathered up a large handful of BrentвАЩs throat, pushed him to the table, and bent him over itвБ†вАФbackвБ†вАФback farther, until the purple neck was corded with distended veins, and his laboured breathing signed it was a good time to ease up.

вАЬLike to talk business now?вАЭ

Brent raised heavily to one elbow, and his hand fumbled in the desk drawer.

вАЬDrop that gun!вАЭ Merrick closed down on the wrist until BrentвАЩs fingers released their hold on the automatic. It fell to the floor. вАЬThat shooting project costs you extra.вАЭ Once more BrentвАЩs AdamвАЩs apple and environs were compressed until his breath came in agonizing little whistles.

Merrick stooped and picked up the gun, emptied it, pocketed the cartridges, and waited for his host to recover.

After some minutes he sat up, rubbed his bloodshot eyes clumsily with his fists, and felt gingerly of his neck.

вАЬWell,вАЭ he croaked, вАЬnow youвАЩve proved youвАЩre bigger than I am, what do you want?вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm no bigger than you are, Brent. Your trouble was that you rushed me with your eyes shut. Just took a long chance that maybe, somehow, your blow would land. ThatвАЩs probably what ails you, all along the line. You close your eyes and whang away, hoping youвАЩll strike something by accidentвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ That afterthought of yours about the gunвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ youвАЩd be in a nice mess by now if I had let you shoot meвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Some little gambler, you are!вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩd rather take a chance on the chair than the pen!вАЭ

вАЬThat proves what IвАЩm sayingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ AndвБ†вАФspeaking of chairs and pens, crawl over there to your desk and sit down. IвАЩm going to have you write something for me.вАЭ

вАЬIf you think youвАЩre going to extort a confession from me, Merrick, youвАЩve another think coming.вАЭ

вАЬConfession? Nonsense! IвАЩve enough evidence in my pocket to send you up for twenty yearsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I want you to write a letter to your cousin. IвАЩll dictate it. DonвАЩt make any mistakes in it, for IвАЩm going to mail it myself.вАЭ

Brent slumped off the table and sweeping the desk clear of its litter took up a pen.

вАЬBegin with her address and your customary salutation. Continue as follows: In view of the uncertainty of your income from your Northwestern Copper stock, I have disposed of itвБ†вАФвАЭ

Flinging the pen down, Brent shouted, вАЬDo you realize youвАЩre forcing me to write this letter?вАЭ

вАЬOh, rather! ThatвАЩs why youвАЩre writing it! Proceed, please.вАЭ

вАЬDo you know what that is?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ compelling me to write this?вАЭ

вАЬWhat is it?вАЭ

вАЬWhyвБ†вАФitвАЩsвБ†вАФitвАЩsвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬYou think up the name for it after IвАЩm goneвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Pick up that pen!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ YouвАЩve got that about your disposal of her Northwestern Copper? NowвБ†вАФgo onвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I have taken its equivalent for you in Axion Motor Corporation, preferredвБ†вАФвАЭ

Brent hesitated and glanced up mystifiedly.

вАЬI donвАЩt understand!вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs unimportant,вАЭ said Merrick crisply. вАЬItвАЩs none of your business, in fact. YouвАЩre just acting as my clerk for the moment. You have nothing further to do in your cousinвАЩs business affairs after writing this note:вБ†вАФвАШAxion Motor Corporation, preferred; five hundred shares listed today at two hundred and twenty-six. This stock is held by the Trust Department of the Fourth National Bank of Detroit from where your dividends will hereafter be remitted to you regularly.вАЩвАКвАЭ

вАЬHow do I know thatвАЩs true?вАЭ growled Brent.

вАЬYou donвАЩt; butвБ†вАФas I saidвБ†вАФitвАЩs no affair of yours. Have you written it? NowвБ†вАФone more paragraph:вБ†вАФI find that an important business errand requires me to go immediately to Buenos AiresвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬButвБ†вАФIвАЩm not going to Buenos Aires!вАЭ

вАЬOh, yes, you are!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Sailing on SaturdayвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ YouвАЩre just packing up, now, to leave for Washington to get the passport IвАЩve arranged for you. From there you go to New York where you sail on the VigoвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Get on now with your correspondence:вБ†вАФвАШI do not know how long I shall be away; so I am transferring my entire responsibility for your business to the Trust Department of the Fourth National Bank of Detroit. Mr.¬†T. P. Randall will verify this and give you a clear statement of your affairs in a short timeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЩ Now add whatever pleasant amenities you may have the crust to write to a woman you have robbed, and sign your name to itвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and address an envelope.вАЭ

While he wrote, Merrick took out his wallet, extracted a steamship ticket, and counted two thousand dollars in bills of large denomination.

вАЬThere.вАЭ He pointed to it, as Brent finished. вАЬThat is for you. Take itвБ†вАФand be off! And if youвАЩre in the market for any advice, IвАЩd suggest that you quit trying to be a sport, which you most certainly arenвАЩt; make some new connections; find a few honest friends; get yourself a respectable job; buck upвБ†вАФand be a man!вАЭ

His face distorted, Brent fumbled with the money, and blindly groped out with his hand. Merrick ignored the gesture. He was not fond of the movies.

вАЬYou can get out of the country, canвАЩt you?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ IвАЩve fixed things for you so youвАЩll have no trouble about the passport, unless youвАЩre wanted for some other crimeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Sure youвАЩll not be stopped?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ HavenвАЩt been robbing anybody else besides your little cousin, have you?вАЭ

вАЬNo! No!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ GodвБ†вАФbut IвАЩve been a rotter!вАЭ Collapsing in his chair, Monty buried his tousled head in his arms.

вАЬYou donвАЩt have to remain a rotter, BrentвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Start fresh!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Go straight!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ You can do it!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ IвАЩm going now. If you have any trouble getting off, wire me. ThereвАЩs my card. And I presume I neednвАЩt tell you that Mrs.¬†Hudson is never to know anything about this little transaction of ours.вАЭ

вАЬYou mean,вАЭ said Brent, looking up perplexed, вАЬthat sheвАЩs not going to know youвАЩve given her all this money? What do you expect to get out of it?вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs my business!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ NoвБ†вАФI think IвАЩll tell youвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ just to clear your mind of any nasty suspicion that my obligation to her may be somehow to her discreditвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Do you recall the story of her husbandвАЩs death?вАЭ

Brent nodded.

вАЬDo you remember that a young man was resuscitated, while Doctor Hudson was drowning?вАЭ

вАЬYeahвБ†вАФI rememberвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ some rich guyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ By GodвБ†вАФit was you?вАЭ

вАЬYes! My life was saved, that day, by a machine which might have brought Doctor Hudson around, if heвАЩd had a chance at itвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Had Hudson lived, you would not have squandered Mrs.¬†HudsonвАЩs moneyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Do you understand now?вАЭ

вАЬWas that why you became a doctor too?вАЭ inquired Brent, wide-eyed.

вАЬThat would be a good enough reasonвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ butвБ†вАФno matter about thatвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I just wanted you to know the nature of my interest in your cousinвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I donвАЩt give a damn what you think about me, but IвАЩd prefer youвАЩd think straight about her!вАЭ

вАЬSoвБ†вАФyou figured he had handed your life back to youвБ†вАФsort ofвБ†вАФand you had to make good with it; is that it?вАЭ

вАЬSomething like that.вАЭ

For a long time Brent sat staring up glassily, and when he spoke his voice came from a distance.

вАЬGod!вБ†вАФI never heard of such a thing!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And here youвАЩve just handed my life back to me!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ If I were the kind of a person you are, I suppose IвАЩd have to do something about it, wouldnвАЩt I?вАЭ

вАЬOhвБ†вАФnot necessarilyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I was a good while debating whether to do anything about my affairвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I was a pretty rummy lot, BrentвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ IвАЩd have ducked it if I could.вАЭ

вАЬHow do you meanвБ†вАФrummy?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ HadnвАЩt stolen anything, had you?вАЭ

вАЬNever needed to steal anythingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Had everythingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Had too much!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Corrupted a lot of people with it!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ YouвАЩve had too little. I suppose thatвАЩs the main difference between us.вАЭ

вАЬDвАЩyou think thereвАЩs anything I could doвБ†вАФto square up for what youвАЩve done for me?вАЭ

вАЬPerhapsвБ†вАФif that sort of thing interests you?вАЭ

вАЬWhatвБ†вАФfor instance?вАЭ

Merrick rose and took up his coat.

вАЬYouвАЩll have to figure that out for yourself.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩd like to, you know,вАЭ said Brent earnestly.

вАЬYou mean that?вАЭ challenged Merrick, putting both hands on MontyвАЩs shoulders and looking him squarely in the eyes.

вАЬYesвБ†вАФmore than I ever meant anything in my life!вАЭ

It seemed to Monty that his athletic young benefactor would never emerge from the brown study into which his words had driven him. He stood, leaning against the table, hands deep in his pockets, oblivious to his surroundings.

вАЬIn that case,вАЭ he said slowly, вАЬI presume IвАЩll have to help you. I donвАЩt want to! ButвБ†вАФthereвАЩs just a slim chance thatвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ See here! You shave and dress for dinner. IвАЩll be in the lobby when you come down. WeвАЩll eatвБ†вАФand IвАЩll tell you all I know about it. After thatвБ†вАФit will be up to you!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Better put some iodine on those scratchesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Sorry I hurt you.вАЭ

They went in a taxi to a down town restaurant. It was somewhat after the dinner hour and their table in the corner permitted them to talk undisturbed. Counselled to forget the experience of the past two hours, Monty had regained his self-confidence and listened with rapt attention to his host. If it was not good soil for the reception of a new idea, it was for no lack of sufficient plowing and harrowing.

вАЬI am going to tell you a strange story, Brent, about a sculptor named Randolph. If it seems incredible to you, I shall not be surprised. It was to me when I first heard it.вАЭ

It was a long story. Dinner courses came and went. In the hour, Monty had spoken but once.

вАЬHeвАЩd quite lost his mind, hadnвАЩt he?вАЭ

Dessert glasses had now been pushed aside and cigars lighted.

вАЬOf course,вАЭ Merrick was saying, вАЬif you should decide to experiment with this projection of yourself through investments in other people, you must be prepared for all manner of failures, disillusionments, disgusts. You will frequently go to no end of trouble and expense for somebody who turns out to be a pest and a piker. You will be imposed upon, lied to, lied about! You will run into cases of ingratitude so rank that it will sicken you! ButвБ†вАФnow and again, you will manage to put the thing overвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and when you do you will discover it has squared for all the failures youвАЩve had!вАЭ

He paused, and his mood seemed reminiscent.

вАЬI expect youвАЩre wondering,вАЭ risked Monty, вАЬwhether youвАЩve been wasting your time and money on me.вАЭ

вАЬSo far as the moneyвАЩs concerned, IвАЩm afraid that wasnвАЩt an investment in you. I just wanted to be rid of you. ButвБ†вАФyouвАЩre quite right about my wondering whether itвАЩs really been worth while to confide to you this theory of personality projection.вАЭ

вАЬDo you mind if I inquire whether you think it would give you additional personal power in your undertakings, if I managed to make good?вАЭ

вАЬOh, unquestionably!вАЭ

вАЬAndвБ†вАФif it ever got outвБ†вАФthat you had started me on the way upвБ†вАФthen this whole investment of yours in my behalf would be a total loss to you?вАЭ

Merrick smiled and toyed preoccupiedly with his ashtray.

вАЬWellвБ†вАФno!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Randolph was a bit obsessed, you know. He was for breaking peopleвАЩs necks if they told of his investments in them. That was going it rather stronger than necessaryвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Hudson was careful to caution his beneficiaries against telling, and threw every possible safeguard around his investments to protect them from discovery; but I donвАЩt believe he felt they hadnвАЩt been worth doing if the facts leaked outвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The really important feature of it is here: if you succeed in expanding your personality, I shall come in on the reward by just as much as IвАЩve been responsible for itвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ In the process of expanding yourself, you are almost sure to help somebody else make himself bigger. He, in turn, energizes other peopleвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ If you know anything about chemistry, you will be helped by considering this as a process of catalysisвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Personality projection is like any other investment. The thing goes on! It earns compound interest. If you were the agent that set it going, the creditвАЩs yoursвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Some of it will be going at full blast long after you have been declared dead. In actual fact, the real you may be more alive, as to personality energy, fifty years after youвАЩre gone, than when you seem to be at the top of your power!вАЭ

вАЬButвБ†вАФif your beneficiary does not succeed in making good on your investment?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Merrick shrugged.

вАЬIn that case, you can see for yourself that nothing comes of it.вАЭ

вАЬButвБ†вАФyou tried! IsnвАЩt there any satisfaction to be had of that?вАЭ

вАЬOh, itвАЩs good practice, I supposeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ ButвБ†вАФif you had spent twenty thousand dollars and six monthsвАЩ time sinking an oil-shaft, and got nothing but a dry hole, would you have much satisfaction in reflecting thatвБ†вАФat all eventsвБ†вАФyouвАЩd tried?вАЭ

When they parted at the kerb, Brent said, вАЬMay I write to you, sometimes, and report?вАЭ

вАЬGlad to have youвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But you neednвАЩt try to tell me what youвАЩre doing for anybody else. ThatвАЩs your affair. Write and tell me if it worksвБ†вАФbut not what you did to make it work. Do you get me?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Good luck!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Goodbye!вАЭ

Bobby went to the Ritz-Carlton for the night, stood for a long time at the counter of the Western Union office in the lobby trying to compose a cable to Vienna, gave it up, bought a few magazines and took the elevator.

Having made himself comfortable in a dressing-gown, he drew up to his desk and wrote:

вАЬDear Marion: IвАЩve been racking my brain for a solution to your problem, but nothing comes. You have lost a friend and I see no way for you to regain her at present. I learned tonight that she is in Nice. But I donвАЩt want you to go there seeking a reconciliation, for you wouldnвАЩt do it without making things bad all around. You would be obliged to say that you went to Bellagio as my agent to discover what had happened to her money. IвАЩve arrangedвБ†вАФguided by your reportвБ†вАФfor the complete untangling of her affairs. She has recovered in full. But the machinery devised to effect this restoration of her property without offence to her is frail enough. I believe and hope it will go, but it wonвАЩt stand any wrenches being tossed into it.

вАЬThe postscript to your letter broke me all up. She left Bellagio about four, you saidвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ drenching rainвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ heartsick and betrayedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ by you and meвБ†вАФwho would have laid down and died for her!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Stuffy little steamer to ComoвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Probably spent the night thereвБ†вАФor maybe caught a train over to MilanвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ wondering where to goвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ what to do next! My dearвБ†вАФwas there ever a more pitiful state of things?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Consult Jack about this. Ask him if he sees any other way out. If neither you nor he can think of a plan by which you can communicate with her without jeopardizing everything we have tried to do for her, better keep away! IвАЩm devastated over the situation, butвБ†вАФthere you are!вАЭ

Then he wrote a letter to Helen which he had no intention of mailing, tore it into small bits, undressed, went to bed, tried to read, turned out the light, relaxed.

For a long time it had been his custom, just before dropping off, to attempt an inward look. His corridorвБ†вАФas he called itвБ†вАФwas, of course, a mere hallucination developed and encouraged by his own quest of it. He had long since decided that the corridor was but an eccentric property of his own imagination, located somewhere in that No-ManвАЩs-Land between fading consciousness and sleep.

It amused him to search for it, and, by practice, he had been able to arrest the clouding of his consciousness at the exact phase where his curious phantom resided.

The clearness of it depended upon his mood; and his moodвБ†вАФin respect to the corridorвБ†вАФwas determined by the projects he happened to be working on in the field of personality projection.

Usually a very thin, faint pencil of promising yellow light streaked down the middle of the corridorвАЩs rough flaggingвБ†вАФthe flagging was always rough as if paved with cobblestones. There would be but an instant of it. The big doors would part, and the light would shine throughвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ just enough to nourish a great hope.

TonightвБ†вАФperhaps because of the investment he had made, his intense concentration upon the subject he had endeavoured to make clear to Brent, and the emotional strain incident to bothвБ†вАФhis mood, he found, was unusually conducive to a materialization of the corridor.

As he neared that grey twilight of consciousness, it came sharply into focus. The doors, instead of parting a little, slowly, tentatively, were opening! The corridor was flooded with a shimmering radiance.

After that, events moved with bewildering swiftness. The corridor suddenly seemed objectifiedвБ†вАФa thing apart from himselfвБ†вАФand he walked into it! A terrific roar deafened himвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Finding the blinding glare at the big doors too painful to face, he turned his attention to the objects against the wall, blinking in his effort to accommodate his eyes to the dazzling light.

All endeavours to recall, afterwards, exactly what he saw there were futile. They belonged to a narrowly restricted phase of half-consciousness, and were not to be reconstructed elsewhere. He was left only with a very hazy impression that he had seen his own laboratoryвБ†вАФthe oven, the black switchboard, the little vice screwed to the table. His diminutive blast-furnace was at top heat. White flames jutted out about the hinges. Doubtless that accounted for the roar. There was also a nebulous recollection left that the door of the five-foot cabinet, containing all the apparatus he had been at such pains to manufacture over a period of many months, stood wide openвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He had almost decided, a few days earlier, to dismantle it and have it carted off before some inquisitive colleague in the hospital discovered what an audacious thing he had had in mind, and chaffed him aboutвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

WellвБ†вАФbe he waking or sleeping, sane or crazyвБ†вАФthere it was!

In the lowest compartment there was a box containing the vacuum tubes; but they were not arranged in the order of the tubes in his cabinet!

He had summoned all his efforts to concentrate on that illusory tube-box, and the exertion aroused him to full consciousness.

Tossing aside the bedclothes, he leaped out drenched with perspiration and trembling so he could barely stand. For an hour he sat at his desk, drawing diagrams of another experimental hookup of his tubes. He was unable to shake off the impression that he was on the edge of discovery. A strange sensation of exultancy possessed him.

Mechanically putting on his street clothes, he went down into the deserted lobby and sought the outer air. For miles and miles he walked, neither knowing nor caring where he went; walked with long strides, seeing nothing; utterly absorbed by the curious experience that still clung to him like a garmentвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ When dawn broke, he found himself down at the ferry-docks.

Returning to the hotel, he bathed, breakfasted, and drove to the station. Securing a compartment, he went to bed and slept dreamlessly all day. When he awoke it was dark, and for a moment he was unable to recall where he was. Then remembrance came, and he smiled broadly. A strange sense of mastery exalted him. He laughed, and recalled Randolph. Randolph had laughed. Randolph had found the grass greener; everything tuned up to a higher key; every sensation more intense. He laughed as Randolph had laughed!

вАЬAnd once I thought him crazy!вАЭ

He sat on the edge of his berth and stared hard at the shiny mahogany walls of his compartment, his eyes wide with the interest of his pleasurable self-analysis. He laughed.

вАЬAnd once I thought Hudson was crazy!вАЭ

The jolting of the trucks over the rail-ends, the clank of chains, and the wail of flanges tortured by a sharp curve, stirred him out of his rhapsody. The sound of his own laughter still echoed in his brain. He rubbed his forehead roughly with the back of his wrist, and swallowed with a dry throat.

вАЬMy God!вАЭ he groaned. вАЬI wonder if IвАЩm going mad!вАЭ
